FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2-4 follow-up (N=7691), ages 12 years at Year 2; 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using GSEM with sequential modeling approaches; estimates direct and indirect effects

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (IV, Year 2) paired with cannabis experimentation (DV, Year 4). Cannabis expectancies (mediator, Year 3) measure individual beliefs. Both smartphone/SM exposure and cannabis use are individual-trait-level constructs

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential mediation analysis reports numeric results: direct effect B=0.18 (95% CI: 0.13-0.24, p<0.001); indirect effects via positive expectancies (B=0.05, p<0.001) and negative expectancies (B=0.03, p<0.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: regression beta coefficients, indirect effect estimates (B values), 95% confidence intervals, percentages mediated (19.8% and 13.6%)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design with SM as IV (Year 2 predicting Year 4). Paper includes analysis with SM-as-IV, therefore FT-EC7 does not apply
notes: Clean prospective design with temporal precedence. Cascade: all MET through FT-IC4; FT-EC7 does not apply (SM-as-IV analysis present). INCLUDE

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2-3 follow-up (N=9799), ages 11-12 years at Year 2; 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with linear and logistic regression models examining prospective associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Lancet Regional Health: Americas, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyberbullying victimization as standalone exposure with no separately-measured smartphone or social media variable. Per criteria: 'Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable) does NOT qualify.' Outcomes are mental health and substance use (individual-trait-level), but the qualifying exposure must be smartphone/SM paired with these outcomes, not cyberbullying alone

notes: Fails FT-IC2: no qualifying SM/phone variable; cyberbullying alone does not meet the exposure requirement. Exclusion code FT-EC2. EXCLUDE

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 (2019-2021), cross-sectional analysis of N=10,305 early adolescents ages 12-13 years; 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple regression and modified Poisson regression to estimate associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Sleep Health: Journal of the National Sleep Foundation, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary IV is 'bedtime screen use mean score' calculated as the average across 7 device/activity types: movies/videos/TV, video games, music, phone/text, social media, internet browsing, and computer/laptop. This is a multi-device/multi-activity composite bundling TV, gaming, phone, and computer use with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM alone. Per criteria: 'Composite "screen time" totals that bundle TV/console/phone with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM do NOT qualify.' The measure does not isolate phone/SM; it aggregates disparate modalities

notes: Fails FT-IC2: composite bedtime screen measure without per-modality breakdown. Does not isolate phone or SM use. Exclusion code FT-EC2. EXCLUDE
